does: This paper analyzes security vulnerabilities in agentic AI systems through a cybersecurity lens rather than purely an AI safety perspective. It identifies and characterizes threats like prompt injection attacks that enable data exfiltration and unintended behaviors when agents interact with third-party tools and servers. The work systematically examines end-to-end security properties of entire agentic systems rather than isolated AI models, establishing a foundational framework for understanding how traditional cybersecurity principles apply to agents that dynamically interact with external environments. By bridging AI safety and systems security, the paper maps both immediate and longer-term research challenges specific to agent deployment in realistic, threat-aware contexts.
connects: This work builds on two converging research streams: advances in agentic AI systems that enable tool use and autonomous goal-seeking behavior, and decades of classical cybersecurity research on threat modeling, access control, and defense mechanisms. It responds to recent AI safety literature addressing agent reliability and alignment, which has primarily focused on training and behavioral guarantees without considering system-level attack surfaces. The paper extends threat analysis frameworks from systems security—traditionally applied to software, networks, and infrastructure—to the novel domain of AI agents that blur boundaries between model capabilities, tool execution, and external service interaction. It recognizes that prompt injection parallels established attack vectors like code injection and SQL injection in conventional software security.
enables: This work opens pathways for translating classical cybersecurity defenses—isolation techniques, input validation, privilege separation, and monitoring—to agentic AI deployment. It establishes research directions for designing agentic systems with formal security guarantees, developing sandboxing mechanisms that constrain agent tool access, and creating threat models specific to agent-environment interaction. The framework enables future work on security protocols for agent-tool communication, verification of agent compliance with security policies, and resilience mechanisms against adversarial inputs. By grounding agent safety in systems security foundations, it facilitates cross-disciplinary collaboration and could accelerate practical, deployment-ready security solutions for increasingly widespread agentic systems operating in high-stakes domains.
